attributes.stem:
A 39-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-day history of a painful, red lump on her right thigh. She reports it has become increasingly tender and larger. There is a smaller tender red spot adjacent to it in the hair follicle. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She works as an beautician and had waxed her legs last week.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a 2 cm erythematous, tender nodule with a central point on the anterior thigh, with a small similar lesion adjacent. The lesion is firm and warm but non-fluctuant. There is no surrounding cellulitis or lymphadenopathy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a furuncle (boil or bacterial folliculitis), which is a deep infection of the hair follicle usually caused by Staphylococcus aureus. The presentation is typical with a tender, erythematous nodule that develops over several days. It is a common and is not always related to poor hygiene, and can be due to colonisation of Staphylococcus aureus.

Warm compresses are the most appropriate initial management for an uncomplicated furuncle as there are no signs of surrounding cellulitis. Applied 3-4 times daily for 15-20 minutes, they promote spontaneous drainage by drawing the infection to the surface and can provide symptomatic relief. This conservative approach is suitable when there are no signs of systemic illness or surrounding cellulitis, and should be done first-line before antibiotics.

Incision and drainage would be indicated if the furuncle was fluctuant or particularly large (>5 cm). In this case, the lesion is still firm and relatively small, making conservative management more appropriate. If incision and drainage was to be performed, it would likely also require oral antibiotics.

Oral flucloxacillin is not necessary in the absence of surrounding cellulitis, systemic symptoms, or immunocompromise. Systemic antibiotics are reserved for complicated cases or when there is evidence of spreading infection.

Topical mupirocin has minimal utility in treating furuncles as the infection is deep within the dermis. It may be useful for preventing recurrence to reduce the bacteria in carriers by applying it to the nostrils, but is not the primary treatment.

Oral doxycycline would be considered in cases of recurrent furunculosis or methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) infection, neither of which is suggested in this case.
learningPoints: Simple furuncles can be managed conservatively with warm compresses and good hygiene.
Systemic antibiotics are only required if there are signs of surrounding cellulitis or systemic symptoms.
references: Oakley A, Ngan V, Gomez J.  Boil. DermNet NZ. 2016.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/boil

Spurling G, Askew D, King D, Mitchell G.  Bacterial skin infections: An Observational study.  Australian Family Physician. 2009: 38 (7).  Available: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/2a153546-6ce9-467b-9a50-938682cf21ca/Bacterial-skin-infections.aspx
